caption: Most entrepreneurs waste 6 months on setup While you're perfecting your website, competitors are getting customers. Launch first, perfect later.
Technical debt starts at zero Every feature you build without customers is a guess. Start simple, grow based on real feedback.
Revenue beats perfection A $500 imperfect business beats a $0 perfect idea every time. Start earning, then improving.
Your MVP is probably too complex The minimum viable product should embarrass you. If it doesn't, you're overthinking it.
Build time: 2 hours vs 2 months Stop coding what you can customize. Focus your energy on what makes you unique.
Day 1 vs Day 100 Day 1: Get something online. Day 100: Make it better. Most people never leave day 0.
Validation requires visitors You can't validate an idea that no one can see. Get online first, then optimize for conversion.
Speed is a competitive advantage In business, being first matters more than being perfect. Launch fast, iterate faster.
Stop building features Most startups fail because they build what customers don't want. Start selling, then building.
Your business plan is wrong No plan survives first customer contact. Start with assumptions, not detailed projections.
Technical founders quit faster Coding your way out of customer problems is procrastination disguised as productivity.
Unique ideas usually fail If no one's doing it, there might be a reason. Start with proven models, then innovate.
More features = slower growth Complexity kills conversion. Every extra feature is another reason for customers to hesitate.
Passion doesn't pay bills Follow the market, not your passion. You can be passionate about profitable problems.
Perfect timing is a myth You'll never feel ready. The market will never be perfect. Start anyway.
